behaviors.description: Manifest validation rejects plugins with missing required fields before loading code
behaviors.justification: This is deterministic validation logic with no external I/O or concurrency. A Unit test can mock the manifest JSON (or pass objects directly to a validation function) and mock the dynamic import to verify it's never called. The core failure mode—validation logic not checking required fields, or import() being called despite validation failure—is directly testable with mocks.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the manifest loading to provide JSON with missing required fields (e.g., no 'name' or 'version'), pass it to the validation function, verify validation throws an error with a clear message, and verify that import() is never called for that plugin. Test edge cases: empty manifest, manifest with null required fields, partially missing fields.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test is achievable at Unit level with mocked manifests and import() spies. Real filesystem interaction is not required to catch the core failure.

behaviors.description: Plugin dependency ordering is respected (dependee loads before dependent)
behaviors.justification: This is graph-algorithm logic—dependency resolution and topological sorting. No I/O, no runtime side effects needed to verify ordering. A Unit test can mock manifests with declared dependencies and mock the onLoad hooks, then verify the call sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Mock manifests for plugins A, B, C where A→B (A depends on B) and B→C (B depends on C). Mock the onLoad hooks as spies. Call the loader. Verify onLoad was called in order: C, then B, then A. Test edge cases: diamond dependency (A→B, A→C, B→D, C→D), single plugin with no dependencies, long chains.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Ordering is a deterministic property of the resolution algorithm. Mocks are sufficient; no real hooks or side effects needed.

behaviors.description: Circular plugin dependencies are detected and reported; neither plugin loads
behaviors.justification: Cycle detection is pure graph algorithm logic. No I/O or runtime behavior is involved. A Unit test can mock manifests with circular dependencies and verify the loader detects the cycle, reports it, and doesn't load either plugin.
behaviors.test_description: Mock manifests where A→B and B→A (circular dependency). Call loader. Verify loader throws or returns an error identifying the cycle (e.g., 'Circular dependency: A → B → A'). Verify neither A nor B's onLoad hook is called. Test edge cases: self-loop (A→A), 3-way cycle (A→B→C→A), cycle with additional branches.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Cycle detection is deterministic. Mocks are sufficient to verify the algorithm works.

behaviors.description: Plugin onLoad hook is invoked only after the entry module has been imported and executed
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on verifying that code at module scope (top-level code in the entry file) actually executes before the hook. Mocking import() hides whether the module is truly executed. A real plugin file with observable side effects is needed. Integration-level test using real filesystem and import() can verify the module code runs (via a module-level side effect like a global variable or function) before the onLoad hook is called.
behaviors.test_description: Create real plugin files in a temp directory where the entry module sets a global variable (e.g., `globalThis.pluginALoaded = true`) at module scope. Mock the plugin export to include an onLoad hook. Use real import() (or require()) to load the plugin. Verify the global variable is set *before* onLoad is called (can track timing or use a spy on onLoad that checks the variable exists). Test edge cases: module with side effects in nested requires, module with async initialization, module that throws during import.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Real module execution must be verified; mocking import() would miss cases where the module isn't actually loaded. Integration-level with real files and imports is necessary.

behaviors.description: When a plugin throws during onLoad, remaining independent plugins continue loading
behaviors.justification: This is error handling and control flow logic. The failure mode—loader crashes or stops, or loads plugins that depend on the failed plugin—is deterministic and testable with mocks. No real I/O or side effects are required. Unit tests can mock manifests, mock onLoad to throw, and verify the loader catches the error and continues with plugins that don't depend on the failed one.
behaviors.test_description: Mock manifests for plugins A, B, C where A has no dependencies, B→A (depends on A), C has no dependencies. Mock B's onLoad to throw an error. Call loader. Verify: (1) loader catches the error and logs/reports it, (2) A's onLoad is called, (3) C's onLoad is called, (4) B's onLoad is called and throws. Test edge cases: multiple plugins throw errors, error thrown in middle of dependency chain, early plugins fail and later independents still load.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Error handling and loader resilience are deterministic. Mocks are sufficient; actual hook side effects don't matter for this test.
